title: Polio case and death rates in the United States
subtitle: The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both [wild-](#dod:wild-poliovirus) and [vaccine-derived poliovirus](#dod:vaccine-derived-poliovirus) infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.
caption:
Polio case and death rates in the United States
The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both [wild-](#dod:wild-poliovirus) and [vaccine-derived poliovirus](#dod:vaccine-derived-poliovirus) infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.

This line chart tracks the reported polio case rate and polio death rate in the United States from 1910 through 2023, with the vertical axis measuring the number of cases or deaths per 100,000 people. The data displays two distinct series: the polio case rate, shown in orange, and the polio death rate, shown in blue. For the first half of the century, the case rate fluctuates significantly, with a notable spike reaching approximately 28 per 100,000 around 1916. Following a period of lower activity in the 1920s and 1930s, the case rate rises again, culminating in the chart's absolute maximum of roughly 36 per 100,000 in the early 1950s. The death rate follows a similar trajectory but remains consistently lower, peaking at approximately 7 per 100,000 during the same mid-century surge. After 1955, both lines show a rapid and sustained decline. By 1960, the case rate drops below 5 per 100,000, and shortly thereafter, both the case and death rates fall to zero. From the early 1960s to the end of the timeline in 2023, both lines remain flat along the horizontal axis at the zero mark. The visual record shows a transition from high volatility and periodic epidemics in the first half of the 20th century to a complete absence of reported cases and deaths in the latter half.
